item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be sourced. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The picture of the bookcase is making me feel really attacked right now.

I'm in this picture and I don't like it.

:-)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoting for the core idea, but I have to say, the missing person seems tacked in to make it 'spooky'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This NEEDS a test log. I never thought I would ever say it. Other than that, upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 for shout out to Worcester, Mass, as well as being an all-around solid article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hi, thanks for reading it. I haven't been really active in a long time (one year since my last contribution) but I try to visit the site once in a while. I don't think I will ever modify any SCPs, but I was planning to eventually write some Tales about these books.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know if you're really active and all anymore, but I REALLY like the meiotic book concept. Unfortunately there's not enough here for me to give an upvote :/. If you add some examples of various books and the results, short summaries of what the result is (4-5) you definitely have my upvote and I'll pass it around with my friends. Regardless, it's still something I'll be checking back in on to reread every now and again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The eating people thing felt, to me, at odds with the rest of the entry. Until I thought about it as a reshuffling of characters like the ones that occur between the mating books. I like to think that the cat, the maid and Towers have been relocated to the entries adyacent to SCP-1811.

Upvoted for the concept of meiotic books.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted for Lovecraftian Wodehouse. Meiotic books is cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The eating people thing didn't seem developed enough to really add anything. That being said I really like the idea a a guy systematically breeding book, so up voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last diary entry comes across to me as just "babble babble, I be crazy now, babble babble". Given that the earlier entries are rather rational, it's a pretty sharp discontinuity that I could have done without.

Other than that tiny last bit, I think it's great. Judging by the alternating "do a test log" and "don't do a test log" comments, I'd say you've just about hit the sweet spot between giving the audience what they want and always leave 'em wanting more.

For my two cents, I don't think I'd enjoy a full collaborative test log. But a few excerpts of fused text could be entertaining, such as DrOrion has above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, but I upvoted just for the Eldritch Wodehouse.

"He talked about his dreams like some hopeless poet; making me picture a town that looked about the color of a diner in Monte Carlo that had just eaten a bad clam and had an absolutely barmy layout- along with the utterly loopy sayings of some gawdelpus, who kept repeating: "Ms'wrth fhtagn", "Ms'wrth fhtagn."

"Ph'nglui mglw'nafh Ms'wrth Blnd'ngs wgah'nagl fhtagn, old chappie."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ugh. The idea is solid, as others have already said, but I reached the end of the article feeling frustrated and not much else. The explanation of the effect could use a lot of slimming down, as it doesn't really add anything but dead weight. You make an allusion to the possibility of hazardous books, but then never address that point again. The end not is not a bad idea, but it contains virtually nothing of substance. Sorry, but this gets my downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article could really do with some expansion on what you've given us. I was hoping for at least one example excerpt, and was disappointed.

+1, but only barely. You have potential here, I'd very much like to see it expressed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted heartily: I really like the concept, and I especially like that it's interesting without needing a collaborative list of weird titles or anything. We've got too many lists supporting otherwise flimsy articles, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A really good idea… but you don't do enough with it to get an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agree to this. Should make for an interesting read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An example log would be a nice touch

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. This wouldn't be the same without the diary bits. Leave the book mating to those captivated by your idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It could do with more of the guy just casually writing about breeding books like they were dogs, but this is good enough for an upvote.

It doesn't really need an experiment log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My intention was to leave most of it to the imagination of the reader. That's why I mentioned only a few new titles in the diary to give the general idea. I want people to imagine that you could for instance get a book telling you how to open gates for demons. I feel it would be less interesting to say it plainly, but maybe it should be exposed a bit more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! I never tried a collaboration and it would be fun to try one at some point. We'll see what kind of score and comments it gets, but I'll probably re-work it a bit more before adding logs.

Side note: I really like the Master and Margarita allusion.

BTW, my current avatar is a picture of the cover. That's how much I like it, but I'm not done reading it yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvote cause I like the idea, but you should do a bit more. I think a collaborative experiment log would be awesome, to show different examples of the books fusing together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think that's necessary. That's fun in cases like the vending machines where you'd expect the results to be fun or weird, but this concept feels complete in its own right to me. But I'm not the original author.

Side note: I really like the Master and Margarita allusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The process is a transposition of the cell division process called Meiosis involved in sexual reproduction. The beauty to me was that the shelf made the books evolve like living organisms, that grow and reproduce. Maybe I'll simplify it, because it seems a bit long and obtuse.

For the time frame, the idea was that it took him a long time to understand what was happening and then to start using it.
The probability has to be low, so that people won't necessarily notice it all the time. It's a bit like in real life, sometimes you find a book and don't know where it came from. The second date was a typo, because it was supposed to be on the same year. Anyway I will re-think those dates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The extra division didn't make sense to me when I read it. I think it is solid enough with the books merging and then dividing without it growing extra pages out of nowhere.

As for the end log, I actually took it as Balswell became so obsessed with "mating" the books together that he didn't really do anything else. So his maid stopped coming because he forgot to pay her, his cat died because he didn't feed it, and Towers (his agent I assume) just gave up on him. After re-reading it I get that the stuff just disappeared at random, but I like my initial conclusion better. =p.

One thing that I think could use a little more information is the time frame and probability we are talking about here. The reason I say this is because the dates on the journal entries are REALLY far apart. Nearly 2 years go by before a second event happens, meaning that either this writer doesn't keep many books around on his bookshelves (which seems unlikely), or that the probability of an event happening is extremely low.

Overall it is promising but I would like to see more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really unique idea, decently written and with a bit of good extra-articular addition at the bottom.

Of course you realize, this needs a collaborative experiment log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bit like you say I imagined all kind of fun experimental results, but I thought it may get tedious and gimmicky to list dozens of book titles. I thought it may be something I could add later as a log. The point is the new books are not necessarily all interesting: it's natural evolution. It may be good though to give a short list of notable results.

I wasn't sure what kind of ''incident'' to give the owner, and the present version was inspired by The Master and Margarita, an excellent fantasy/satire set during the reign of Stalin, where it was not unusual for people to disappear as if by magic.

First the two books fuse physically into a single book. After shuffling the content, it will grow in size by duplicating it's pages. Somewhat like a living cell replicating it's DNA before dividing. At that point you have a double-size book that will divide in 2 identical books. Each of them re-shuffles to further divide into 2 other books. That way, given enough, you could in principle fill the whole shelf with just 2 books. But maybe I should remove this 2nd division to make it simpler.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I was reading through the description, I expected to see it followed by a list of fused books and summaries of their resulting contents. I'm a little disappointed with what I got instead. If you're going to write a skip that fuses books to make crossover stories, at least have a little fun with idea before launching straight into "it eats people and cats".

Also, maybe I just didn't read it properly, but it wasn't clear to me how the fusions were produced. Do they physically merge together? If so, where the replication come into play?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Awwww! OK, I guess it was premature and I'll work more on it. Thanks for the comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hurm. I kinda like the effect yer going for, but I don't feel like the main article does enough with it to be interesting. I would've left it at a neutral vote, but the logs at the end were narmy and didn't really read like something a real person would write. So, downvote for now, but I hope you continue to work on it, as the idea definitely has promise. I also really like the name, heh.
